name: Aeler
description:
Aeler

Key Demographic: Aeler (Dwarfs)

Captial: Khaz-Veltrum (Arguable)

Entanglements

To the North West:
The Violet Steppe (Ykrul) – The high passes are contested; the Khagan's scouts sometimes ride down the valleys. The mountain holds are a wall, not a welcome.
To the South:
Ecktoria / Vhasia / Viterra / Ubral – The grain barges from the lowlands keep the holds fed; the mountain's appetite is never fully satisfied. The holds trade iron for barley and pray for a mild winter.
To the North East:
The Mistlands (Protectorate) – The bell-lines are an extension of Aeler engineering; ward-salt is paid in iron. The Legate of the Mists answers to the High King.
Across the Aberderrin:
The Valewood / Linns – Star-roads and fey paths occasionally surface in the deep galleries. The Aeler do not dig toward the forest; the forest does not dig toward them.

Quick Reference

Tagline: The mountain remembers every word carved in its stone. The deep remembers every breath we owe.

Vault-Queen of a Crownland (Elite):

“The mountain remembers every seal pressed into its walls, every oath whispered in the dark. To govern here is not to command — it is to listen. The stone sings of what we owe. The deep listens for what we forget.”

Under-Mason (Commoner):

“When the stone starts singing a different tune, you best listen. Three knocks in the morning means all's well. Three knocks at night means something's coming through the walls that wasn't meant to walk. My grandfather called it the pressure that thinks. He walked into the deep one day and never came back. The mountain answered his knock. It always does.”

Genre / Mood: Survival horror underground, infrastructure as soft power, collectivist mercantilism, the weight of every breath.

Starting Location: An Underway junction lit by guttering brass lanterns, its stone walls scored with tally-marks from a dozen forgotten crowns. A Geometer halts the party with a tally-rod. “The Spine shifts,” he says, stone-dust in his beard. “Choose a crown — or be buried by one. The lowland grain barges are late again. If the holds don't eat, the pressure wakes. And if you hear a knock in threes, pretend you did not. The deep is listening tonight.”

Theme & Atmosphere

The Aeler Kingdoms hold the mountains by breath, bell, and bargain. Dozens of sovereign holds — some sealed for centuries, others trading openly with lowland markets — share only the deep roots of stone and the supremacy of the High King Beneath the Peaks. Their halls run like arteries, their cities open in thunderous caverns where bridges leap between ribs of stone and cisterns shine like night skies turned inward. Lore is the first law: air is counted, water is bartered, light is rationed by wick and prayer. Tradition is the second: seals are kept, measures remain true, and the dead keep their offices as carefully as the living. Between the two, a people learn to argue quietly and build loudly.

Collectives — clans, guild-kin, lamp-companies — own what the mountain allows; individuals are custodians by oath. Trade runs under and over the range through under-ways and pass-forts, stitching protectorates and lowland markets to vaultmouth gates. Aeler prosperity depends entirely on surface grain. The holds cannot grow enough food in the dark; each winter, caravans from Acasia, Vhasia, and Viterra bring barley and rye through the high passes. In exchange, the Aeler send iron, copper, and engineered stonework — bridge keystones, mill gears, breath-counter mechanisms. It is a fragile bargain. When the grain is late, the holds ration. When the holds ration, the pressure whispers louder.

But beneath the order, beneath the breath-counters and the record-stones, something older stirs. The Aeler do not speak of it directly. They call it the pressure that thinks — a presence that lives in the deepest galleries, in the spaces between sealed holds, in the darkness where bells have not rung for centuries. It does not attack. It invites. Miners who wander too deep return with no memory of why they went. Others do not return at all. The pressure does not hate. It is patient. And it is always, always listening.

What you notice first: The smell of stale air and hot metal. The constant drip of condensation in the distance — or is it footsteps? The way every Aeler touches a small brass bell at their belt before speaking of the deep. The way they never, ever count to nine aloud.

The one rule that kills newcomers: Never count your breaths aloud in a mine. The mountain listens, and if it hears you reach the ninth, it will take one — a breath, a memory, a shadow. The deep does not forgive miscounts.

Regional Mood: Infrastructure, Famine, and the Pressure Below

Here, power is not measured in gold but in capacity: how many lamp-hours your holding can afford, how many mouths your air-recyclers can support, how many tons of ore you can raise before the grain runs out. The Aeler do not conquer; they engineer. A bridge built to their standards binds a valley to their trade network. A breath-counter installed in a foreign counting house gives them leverage over every contract signed beneath it. Their soft power is written in brass and stone.

Yet the holds are always one bad harvest away from collapse. The Deep Vaults cannot feed themselves. Every season, Edgewalkers — outcasts, rogues, and those too restless for the counting-chamber — are sent to the surface to “find opportunities.” They start feuds between lowland lords, sell weapons to both sides, and return with grain contracts signed in blood. The holds do not ask where the grain came from. They ask only that the wagons arrive before the ninth bell.

The holds endure because they are willing to spend lives as carefully as they spend lamp oil. A collapsed gallery is a line item. A village that starves because its grain was diverted to the Deep Vaults is a footnote in an audit that no one reads aloud. The Aeler are not cruel. They are efficient. And efficiency, in the dark, is the only mercy.

The Pressure That Thinks — The Deep Drake

Deep beneath the oldest mountains, beyond the Ninth Seal and past the last lamplighter's mile, there is a presence. The Aeler do not name it. Among themselves, in whispers, some call it the Deep Drake — an ancient worm, a child of Nidhoggr, hatched in the dark between worlds. But that is speculation, and the Aeler are not a people given to speculation about things they cannot measure.

What they know is this: there is a weight in the deepest stone. It feels like curiosity. It sounds like your own name spoken with concern. It is not a god, not a demon, not a dragon in any sense the surface-dwellers would recognize. It is a proto-patron — a being whose hold on the world is tenuous, whose attention slips like water through fingers. It cannot command. It cannot bless. It can only invite.

The Deep Drake has no lair that any living Aeler has seen, though rumor persists of a vast chamber of black glass and fossilized bone, where the heat of the earth meets the cold of the void. In that chamber, the worm coils around a hoard that is not gold but attention: the still, silent figures of those who walked too far and never walked back. They sit in the dark, eyes open, mouths moving silently as they repeat the last promise they ever made. The Drake does not kill. It collects. And what it collects, it forgets — because its memory is as fragile as its grip on reality. That is why it is always hungry. That is why it is always listening.

The Aeler refuse to think of it as anything other than the pressure that thinks. To name it would be to invite it. To acknowledge it as a being would be to admit that the deep is not merely stone and silence but occupied. So they call it a superstition, a miner's fancy, the kind of story told to frighten apprentices. And for the most part, they believe their own dismissal. But the old miners know. The Edgewalkers who have heard the three knocks and turned back just in time — they know. They do not speak of it. They only count their breaths more carefully.

The Deep Drake draws the living toward it not through force, but through a gentle, irresistible lure: a half-remembered tune, a forgotten oath, a face you thought you had left behind. You walk toward it because you believe you have business there. You have no business there. Its pull is strongest when you are tired, when your attention wanders, when you have stopped counting your breaths. The Aeler have learned to live with this. They seal off tunnels that grow too warm. They never answer a knock that comes in three. They count their breaths not out of habit, but out of a deep, unspoken fear that the ninth breath might be the one the Drake answers.

What the elders do not say, but what the Edgewalkers whisper: The Deep Drake is not the only thing down there. It is a child of something older — Nidhoggr, the corpse-tearer, the one who gnaws at the root of the world. That older thing does not invite. It demands. And it has been patient for a very long time.

The Edgewalker's Bargain

Outcasts from the holds, Edgewalkers are those who could not abide the counting — or who were too useful to execute and too dangerous to keep. They trade in secrets, in weapons, in the subtle art of making two lowland lords need each other's destruction. An Edgewalker's greatest skill is not swordplay but leverage: knowing which noble's grain debt is overdue, which free company captain will flip for a better contract, which bridge toll can be raised to start a war. The holds disavow them publicly and fund them privately. When a war breaks out in the lowlands, Aeler iron flows to both sides. When the war ends, the holds send grain inspectors to collect the tab.

Mechanical hook for Edgewalker characters: Once per arc, an Edgewalker may declare that they have a line on a lowland conflict. The GM creates a 4-segment Opportunity timer. Each tick represents the Edgewalker applying pressure (spreading rumors, selling a weapon, forging a letter). When the timer fills, a war, a feud, or a trade embargo ignites — and the Aeler holds gain +2 Supply from arms sales. The Edgewalker gains 1 Corruption (the Hollow notices their work) and a permanent String on a lowland faction.

The Fractured Crowns: Aeler Faction Spectrum

Aeler society is not monolithic. Beneath the High King's nominal authority, each crownland follows its own star. Attitudes toward the surface range from open partnership to militant isolation, and even the most reclusive holds maintain Edgewalker contacts to monitor the world above.

  • Traditionalist / “The Old Ways” – These holds idealize the ancient mountain-empire: closed gates, iron tariffs, and the certain knowledge that surface-dwellers cannot be trusted. They resemble the dwarves of fireside tales: stubborn, insular, and quick to reach for an axe when a human mentions “progress.” Their kings speak of the days when no foreigner stepped past the vaultmouth. They also speak of the pressure in whispers, and they seal any tunnel that shows signs of warmth.

    Timers: Foreigner Suspicion [4], Hold Sealed [6].

  • Cooperative / “Open Hand” – These holds trade freely with Aelaerem, Kahfagia, and even the Vilikari. They staff the under-markets, send engineers to repair bridges in Viterra, and treat the surface as a resource to be managed, not a threat to be shunned. They prefer soft power: standardized measures, certified weights, and bell-code protocols that make others want to trade on Aeler terms. They are also the most likely to send expeditions into the deep, seeking lost holds — and the most likely to lose miners to the pressure.

    Timers: Trade Route Prosperity [6], Cultural Friction [4].

  • Isolationist / “Silent Stone” – Deep holds that have not answered a Kingsmoot call in living memory. Their gates are lost, their peoples rumored to have turned inward, worshiping forgotten depths — or to have become part of the pressure's hoard. They neither help nor harm the surface — but when a White Flood or a gas flare occurs, their silence becomes a hazard of its own. The Deep Drake is said to lair somewhere beneath the Silent Stone holds.

    Timers: Silent Hold Awakens [8], Ancient Grudge [6].

Most Aeler characters and settlements fall somewhere between these poles, and the same hold might shift its stance over a season, depending on grain prices, a new Vault-Queen, or a disaster that forces its hand.

The Breath Count — Habit, Not Function

Among the Aeler, counting breaths is not merely a safety measure — it is a cultural reflex, as automatic as a human's heartbeat. Aeler children learn to count in eights before they learn to speak. They tap their fingers, their bells, their spoons in rhythms of eight. They never, ever count to nine aloud. The ninth breath is not a number; it is a gap — the space where the pressure listens. Aeler who forget to count in eights are considered unreliable, possibly touched by the deep. Those who deliberately count to nine are shunned; they are thought to have invited the pressure to notice them.

Mechanical note: Breath-counting is not a skill roll or a resource to track. It is a narrative and cultural tic. The GM may use a character's failure to observe the count as a trigger for a complication (e.g., the mountain shifts, a bell rings falsely, a character feels watched). Conversely, an Aeler who scrupulously counts their breaths in eights may gain a small boon (e.g., +1 die to resist fear in confined spaces) as the mountain's attention slides past them.

Role/TitleMotivationRumored Quirk

Grunhelda ThrainsdottirVault-Queen of IrondeepExpand trade routes while keeping the Old Ways satisfiedShe keeps a pet geode that hums when a contract is fair — and goes silent when the pressure is near.
Maeva EmeryLegate of the Under-SealNegotiate passage rights and standard measures with lowland realmsHer seal-rod is carved from a fossilized root. She has never lost a tariff dispute.
Thromm Stone-TongueUnder-Mason (Commoner exemplar)Hear the mountain's warnings and keep the tunnels safeHe can identify a false weight by tapping it once with his hammer. He has never counted to nine.
Orthen White-RibbonTunnel CourierDeliver sealed writs across hostile jurisdictionsHe has run the Spine Underway in darkness without a lamp. Twice. He says the dark has a voice, but it only asks questions.
Lumenor SaelaKeeper of the Under-AltarsMaintain the breath-rites and the old flameShe speaks only in bell tones during services. Her mundane voice is never heard.
Emery “the Unbound”Edgewalker (exiled fixer)Create lowland chaos to secure grain contracts for the holdsShe has three false names and a real one she no longer remembers. She never sleeps in the same room twice.

Names of Aeler

Aeler names blend flowing vowels and soft diphthongs (ae, ei, ou, ui) with harsh consonants (k, th, z, r, g). Surnames are carved from mountain-roots: a hold, a forge, a seam, or a trade.
